findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than 10 instances of 'the way [X] [verb]'. No two consecutive paragraphs use the construction. The backstory section (lines 87-137) contains no more than 4 instances total. At least 3 of the removed instances are replaced with embodied images or direct constructions rather than simple deletion.
findings.evidence: 22 occurrences of 'the way [X] [verb]' across 18 of the chapter's 165 lines. Representative instances: 'the way he dropped mentions of books' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:7), 'the way Nora responded to men who had opinions about books' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:27), 'the way she'd refine any product' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:67), 'the way she was aware of the dirty window...the way she catalogued everything' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:87, two instances on one line), 'the way she held a drink, the way she laughed' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:95, two instances), 'poor in the way that produced...poor in the way that was invisible' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:105, two instances), 'the way a body attached itself to a skeleton' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:107), 'the way people who owed money always thought about money' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:125), 'the way a person driving on a spare tire believed it was fine' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:135), 'the way she kept the personal loan paperwork' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:137).
findings.problem: The classificatory construction 'the way [X] [verb]' appears 22 times in a 165-line chapter — once every 7.5 lines. This is the chapter's dominant prose tic. Simone's analytical, cataloguing consciousness is well-served by this construction in principle, but at this density it flattens the prose into a single rhythmic gear. The reader begins to hear the formula rather than the content. By the backstory section (lines 87-137), where the construction appears on nearly every other paragraph, the prose is running on autopilot. A prize reader would circle this pattern by page three.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'the way [X] [verb]' from 22 instances to no more than 8-10, preserving the strongest and most character-specific uses (e.g., snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:87 'the way she catalogued everything' and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:107 'the way a body attached itself to a skeleton' are earned). The heaviest concentration is in the backstory section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:87-137), which needs the most aggressive revision. Replace cut instances with varied syntax: direct statement, appositive, subordinate clause, or embodied image. For example, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:125 'the way people who owed money always thought about money' could become a direct metaphor or sensory image for involuntary financial vigilance. snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:95 'the way she held a drink, the way she laughed' could be rewritten with specific gesture rather than classification. The goal is syntactic variety that still reads as Simone's consciousness.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than 2 instances of 'the kind of [noun] that,' no more than 3 of 'the specific/particular [noun],' and no more than 1 of 'She was aware.' The three-clause 'She was aware' sequence at line 119 is rewritten so no more than one clause uses that construction.
findings.evidence: Secondary recurring formulas that compound the 'the way' tic: (1) 'the kind of [noun] that [clause]' appears 5 times — 'the kind of face that made women on dating apps think' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:17), 'the kind of name that sounded both casual and textured' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:49), 'the kind of closeness that came braided with guilt' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:69), 'the kind of poor where you owned a house' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:105). (2) 'the specific/particular [noun]' appears 6 times — 'the particular pitch of drunk laughter' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:49), 'the specific warmth of his hand' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:99), 'the specific compound sensation' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:117), 'the specific vigilance' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:125), 'the specific quiet that Williamsburg achieved' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:155). (3) 'She was aware that/of' appears 4 times across lines 87 and 119.
findings.problem: These three secondary patterns — 'the kind of,' 'the specific/particular,' and 'she was aware' — reinforce the same taxonomic prose mode as the primary 'the way' tic. Together with that construction, they create a chapter where Simone's analytical consciousness is rendered almost entirely through classificatory syntax: everything is typed, categorized, and filed. The effect is monotonous. Simone's interiority becomes a single texture — smart-woman-labeling-things — rather than the variable register the style bible describes (shorter and more declarative when alone, brittle under stress, sentences fragmenting).
findings.rewrite_direction: Target the backstory section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:87-121) and the financial section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:125-139), where these secondary patterns cluster most heavily. Reduce 'the kind of' from 5 to no more than 2. Reduce 'the specific/particular' from 6 to no more than 3. Reduce 'She was aware' from 4 to no more than 1. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:105, 'the kind of poor where you owned a house' is strong enough to keep — cut the others. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:119, the three consecutive 'She was aware that' clauses should be collapsed or rewritten so the self-assessment doesn't read as a list. Introduce more sentence-level variety: fragments, direct declarations, images that show rather than classify.

findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains at least 3 instances of profanity in Simone's private narration (not in dialogue or texts to Nora). At least one appears in the financial section (lines 125-131) and at least one in the backstory section (lines 91-121). The profanity reads as natural internal voice, not forced — it lands as punctuation or exhalation, not as a deliberate word choice the character would notice making.
findings.evidence: The style bible specifies Simone's profanity_profile as: 'Liberal. She swears casually and frequently in private thought and solo speech. Uses profanity as punctuation when frustrated. More restrained in public but not artificially clean.' The chapter contains zero profanity in Simone's private narration across ~150 lines of interiority. The only profanity ('holy shit,' snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:21) occurs in a text message to Nora — public register, not private thought. The chapter covers Simone processing jealousy over Jason Park (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:91-99), financial desperation (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:125-131), and the moral weight of her deception (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:133-139) — all contexts where her private voice should register frustration, bitterness, or self-directed anger with characteristic profanity.
findings.problem: Simone's private interiority is scrubbed clean of the profanity the style bible establishes as a core voice marker. This sanitizes her internal register, making her sound more controlled and literary than the character actually is. Her private register should be 'colder, more transactional, more honest about her resentments' — profanity is part of that honesty. Without it, Simone's internal voice reads as authorial essay rather than embodied consciousness. The absence is especially conspicuous in the financial section (lines 125-131), where she's cataloguing $38,000 in debt and $300/month for the rest of her life — a woman who 'uses profanity as punctuation when frustrated' would not process this in clean prose.
findings.rewrite_direction: Add 3-5 instances of natural internal profanity at moments of peak frustration or bitterness. Target spans: (1) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:99 — the Jason Park section, where 'the first time in her life she'd felt chosen' should land with private bitterness, not just wistful precision; (2) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:115 — the layoff-versus-promotion moment, where Simone is sitting in unwashed sweatpants with a loan application open; (3) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:129 — the financial arithmetic section, particularly 'she had roughly three hundred dollars a month for the rest of her life'; (4) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:131 — the salary comparison with Nora. Profanity should be character-natural ('fuck,' 'shit,' 'goddamn'), not performative — it should feel like thought-punctuation, not emphasis.

findings.acceptance_test: The temporal gap stated at line 41 matches the specific dates stated at line 77 within a margin of ±3 days. The chapter's internal timeline is arithmetically consistent.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:41 states: 'Simone had started the Caleb project in the first week of September, six weeks before Nora downloaded Hinge.' snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:77 states: 'She activated the Hinge profile on October 3rd. Nora downloaded Hinge on October 5th.' First week of September (September 1-7) to October 5th is approximately 4 to 5 weeks, not 6. For 'six weeks before October 5th' to be accurate, Simone would have needed to start in late August (approximately August 24), not the first week of September.
findings.problem: The chapter makes two specific temporal claims that don't reconcile arithmetically. Both are narrated as precise facts from Simone's perspective — she's a detail-oriented character who tracks data and timelines. An internal inconsistency of this kind undermines the chapter's portrait of Simone as someone who counts and measures everything. A careful reader will catch the arithmetic.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile the two claims at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:41 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_03.md:77. Options: (A) Change 'six weeks' to 'a month' or 'four weeks' at line 41, which would accurately describe early September to early October. (B) Change 'the first week of September' to 'the last week of August' at line 41, which would make 'six weeks' accurate. (C) Change the Hinge download date at line 77 to October 15-17, which would make 'six weeks from early September' accurate and would align with the continuity sheet's 'Mid-October' for the Caleb match. Option C may be cleanest because it also resolves with the seasonal track (chapters 1-3 span 'early-to-mid October').

findings.acceptance_test: At line 491: (1) No reference to the father dying. (2) The father's departure to Seattle is mentioned. (3) The emotional vulnerability language is preserved — Nora's tenderness, Simone's complicated response. (4) The passage remains consistent with Ch9's version where the father attended NYU graduation and calls on holidays. FAIL if any death reference remains or if the age/circumstance contradicts Ch9 line 1642.
findings.problem: Nora's father is described as having 'died when she was eleven' in Simone's OpenClaw context entry. This contradicts Ch9 (father alive, moved to Seattle at 12), Ch6 (died approximately 4 years ago), and Ch15 (died at 19). Four chapters present four incompatible versions of the father's fate. The Ch9 version — father left for Seattle, alive, attended NYU graduation — is the most dramatically developed and gives the AI's autonomous insight at line 1646 ('the leaving shaped you more than the love did') its full power. Ch3's 'died when she was eleven' should be revised to match the Seattle-departure version.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:491, in the long paragraph of Simone's context entries, change 'Her father, who had died when she was eleven and who she still talked about with a tenderness that made Simone want to hold her or scream' to reflect the canonical version: father left for Seattle when Nora was twelve, is alive but emotionally absent. Replace the death reference with language about the departure and Nora's complicated grief over an abandonment disguised as a career move. Keep the emotional texture — the tenderness Nora shows, Simone's dual impulse to hold or scream — but anchor it to loss-by-leaving rather than loss-by-death. The OpenClaw system would encode this as an emotional vulnerability: a father who chose to leave is arguably more painful than one who died, because the leaving was voluntary.

findings.acceptance_test: At line 447: (1) The name reads 'Marco Henriques,' not 'Miguel Ferreira.' (2) All other details (age, profession, city, follower count) are unchanged. FAIL if 'Miguel Ferreira' remains.
findings.problem: The Lisbon man whose photos Simone uses for Caleb is named 'Miguel Ferreira' in Ch3 (line 447: 'a thirty-year-old architect in Lisbon named Miguel Ferreira') but his Instagram handle in Ch5 is '@marcohenriques_' (line 892), implying the name is 'Marco Henriques.' These are two different names for the same character. The continuity sheet specifies the photos come from 'a real man living in Lisbon' without naming him, so one chapter must correct to match the other.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:447, change 'Miguel Ferreira' to 'Marco Henriques' to match the Instagram handle established in Ch5. The passage reads 'a thirty-year-old architect in Lisbon named Miguel Ferreira, forty-seven followers' — change to 'a thirty-year-old architect in Lisbon named Marco Henriques, forty-seven followers.' This aligns with Ch5's '@marcohenriques_' handle.

findings.acceptance_test: At line 559: (1) Rent amount matches Ch7's $2,400. (2) The total monthly surplus after all expenses is approximately $300, consistent with Ch7 line 1252. (3) The 'raised from' figure creates a plausible increase to $2,400. FAIL if rent is not $2,400 or if the surplus math produces a number inconsistent with Ch7.
findings.problem: Line 559 states Simone's rent is '$2,200, raised from $2,050 in August.' Ch7 line 1242 states rent is '$2,400 a month' in December, with a renewal notice for $2,550 in January. Ch13 line 2516 states rent is '$2,100' in February. Ch3 and Ch7 describe the same pre-renewal period (October and December respectively) but give different amounts ($2,200 vs $2,400). Ch7's financial section is the most detailed and internally consistent, so $2,400 should be treated as the canonical pre-renewal rent. Ch3's $2,200 should be corrected.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:559, change '$2,200, raised from $2,050 in August' to '$2,400, raised from $2,050 in August.' Also verify that the downstream math in this chapter (minimum payments at line 559: '$1,100,' surplus of 'roughly three hundred dollars') still works with $2,400 rent. The current math: $5,800 take-home minus $2,200 rent minus $1,100 minimums minus ~$1,100 expenses = ~$400 surplus. With $2,400 rent: $5,800 - $2,400 - $1,100 - $1,100 = ~$1,200 surplus, which contradicts the 'three hundred dollars' figure. The minimums or expenses may also need adjustment to keep the surplus near $300. Alternatively, use Ch7's exact budget breakdown (line 1250) as the canonical reference and align Ch3's math to it.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its dark comedy register. The humor should be genuinely funny — not arch, not safe, not 'literary funny' where the reader smiles wryly. The darkness should be genuinely dark — the financial manipulation is theft, the emotional manipulation is a form of violence, and the sexual dimension of the AI relationship (intimate photos sent to a non-person, sexual conversations with an algorithm controlled by your best friend) is deeply violating. The novel should not flinch from the specific perversity of Simone reading Nora's intimate messages, of Simone helping Nora dress up for a man Simone invented, of Simone comforting Nora about a fight Simone scripted. These moments are the novel's payload. If they are rendered obliquely or summarized, the book fails. Equally, the genuine tenderness of the friendship — the years of real love underneath the betrayal — must be rendered with the same commitment. The reader should feel both the comedy and the horror simultaneously, not in alternation.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is treated as a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene involves financial desperation, sexual vulnerability, emotional devastation, or the mechanics of deception, the prose must render these with full specificity. 'She felt used' is never acceptable when you can show what being used feels and looks like in the body and in the specific details of the moment. The reader should not be protected from the characters' experiences.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices. If a line reads stiffly, the first fix is to contract.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters. After a high-intensity scene (confrontation, discovery, sexual encounter), the next chapter should breathe — slow down, change the sensory environment, let the character do something ordinary. Vary scene counts: some chapters are a single sustained scene, others are three or four. Vary dialogue density: some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are mostly interiority. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens in a location, the next should open in thought or in mid-conversation. The novel's middle section (chapters 8-13) should have more structural variety than the opening, which can afford to be more regular.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, educated, specific. No genre-thriller diction. No prestige-novel stiffness. The language should feel like how smart, funny, self-aware women in their mid-thirties actually think and speak. Cultural references are allowed when organic. Brand names when they carry social information. The prose should be able to be funny without stepping outside the character's perspective to wink at the reader.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person — very close, almost inhabiting the POV character's thought patterns and verbal rhythms. The prose should feel like the character's consciousness rendered in third person, not an external narrator describing the character. When in Nora's sections, the prose has her warmth and her specific noticing. When in Simone's sections, the prose has her edge and her accounting.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable. Default to mid-length sentences with natural cadence. Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision. Use longer, more digressive rhythms for scenes of intimacy, humor, and daily texture. Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause, almost hidden.
